title: Conviction Isn't Borrowed—It Has to Be Built from Knowledge

source.original: If you want to be early to a trend, you have to go really deep and be an expert – Parker

body_html:
One of the most common failure modes in investing is borrowed conviction. Someone adopts a position based on another person's thesis, holds it through early gains, and then capitulates at the first sign of serious volatility. The reason is straightforward: they never had the foundational understanding required to distinguish between noise and a genuine threat to the thesis.

True conviction—the kind that allows you to hold through 40%, 60%, or even 80% drawdowns—can only come from deep, personal knowledge of the domain. When you understand why something is happening at a structural level, temporary price dislocations become opportunities rather than panic triggers. Without that understanding, every dip feels existential.

This is why being early to a trend and profiting from being early are two very different things. Plenty of people identified Bitcoin, AI, or electric vehicles as important trends years ago. Far fewer had the conviction to maintain their positions through the brutal corrections that inevitably occurred along the way. The ones who did were almost always domain experts who could evaluate each setback in context.

The implication for anyone building a portfolio or making strategic bets is clear: don't take positions you can't defend with first-principles reasoning. If your investment thesis collapses the moment a prominent skeptic publishes a counterargument, you never really had a thesis—you had a hope backed by someone else's confidence.

Building genuine conviction is slow, unglamorous work. It means reading primary sources, understanding technical details, talking to people who actually build in the space, and stress-testing your assumptions repeatedly. But it's the only reliable way to hold through the volatility that separates early entrants from those who merely showed up early and left early.
